Review

A systematic review of fish trade and its impact on economic progress: Barriers, opportunities, and outlook for developing countries
============================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Ehsan Elahi a, Yuge Li a, Ali Md Emam a, Min Zhu a, Indubala Krishnan b

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ocecoaman.2024.107471](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ocecoaman.2024.107471 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0964569124004563&orderBeanReset=true)

Highlights
----------

*   •The study evaluates fishery commerce's impact in developing nations. 
*   •It uses a systematic literature review and desk research. 
*   •Findings identify rising global fish demand due to health and dietary shifts. 
*   •Developing nations found growth potential due to notably increased fish market share. 
*   •Sustainability & collaboration are essential for long-term fisheries growth. 

Abstract
--------

This study conducts a comprehensive evaluation of fishery commerce effects, with a particular emphasis on developing nations, through a systematic literature review and desk research from 2012 to 2024. The study provides a detailed assessment of the fish trade landscape in these regions by analyzing existing data, official documents, and empirical research. The research reveals a growing global demand for fish, driven by changing dietary preferences and increased health awareness. However, significant challenges impede the sustainable development of the fisheries sector in developing countries. Despite these obstacles, data visualization demonstrates a remarkable increase in developing countries' participation in the global fish market in terms of value and volume. The study identifies substantial untapped potential for these nations to expand their fisheries industry, thereby fostering broader economic growth. Realizing this potential requires a strong emphasis on sustainability, achievable through modernization, stringent regulations, infrastructure improvements, and biodiversity preservation to secure long-term marine investments. The research underscores the importance of engaging all stakeholders in the fishing industry – including workers, shareholders, management bodies, sustainability specialists, and government officials – in developing transparent national and regional policies. This inclusive approach facilitates open dialogue about objectives and necessary trade-offs. The study concludes that sustainable practices and policies in the fish and fisheries sector must be carefully tailored to the specific limitations and advantages of developing countries to ensure balanced and favorable outcomes in future scenarios.

Introduction
------------

Fish exports have emerged as a critical component in the economic advancement and sustainable development of developing nations (Muñiz et al., 2022; Emam et al., 2021; Golub and Verma, 2014). The growing global recognition of fish as a sustainable nutrition source (Duggan and Kochen, 2016) and its status as a natural superfood (Obiero et al., 2019) underscore the importance of understanding the current economic landscape and identifying potential growth opportunities within the fisheries sector. The increasing demand for fish is driven by a confluence of factors, including evolving dietary preferences, heightened health consciousness, rising incomes, and rapid urbanization (Anderson et al., 2017; Tran et al., 2023). Technological advancements have further catalyzed this demand, particularly in emerging markets, outpacing growth in developed countries (FAO, 2020). Consequently, developing nations find themselves at the epicenter of this expanding market (Kumar et al., 2019). Beyond their nutritional significance, fish exports have become a cornerstone of global trade, ranking among the most widely traded commodities worldwide (Muñiz et al., 2022; Golub and Verma, 2014; Asche et al., 2015) and constituting approximately 10% of all agricultural product exports (UNCTAD, 2017).

The scale of this trade is substantial. According to the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), global fish exports reached 38.7 million tons in 2018, with a trade value surpassing USD 164 billion (FAO, 2020). Developing countries have made significant strides in this market, generating USD 75 billion from aquatic product exports in 2020, accounting for 54% of the total volume, equivalent to 32 million tons of globally exported fish (FAO, 2022). Nations such as Vietnam and Thailand have emerged as key players, highlighting the sector's economic potential (FAO, 2020). The international fish export market is predominantly shaped by developing countries, with China and India leading the charge due to their consistent growth in both capture and aquaculture fish production (Nisar et al., 2021). China's trade value soared to an impressive USD 39.36 billion in 2019 (China Agriculture Press, 2020), representing nearly 9% of global fish imports and 14% of fish exports by value (FAO, 2020). This underscores the pivotal role of developing countries in the global fish trade (UNCTAD, 2017), largely attributed to their comparative advantage in possessing abundant water resources capable of supporting a diverse range of fish species (Golub and Verma, 2014).

Fish exports have become a cornerstone of economic development and sustainability for many developing nations and small island states (Asche et al., 2015; Kumar et al., 2020). These exports contribute significantly to local economies and human welfare by generating employment opportunities throughout the value chain, from production to trade and marketing of both farmed and wild fish (Muñiz et al., 2022; Kumar et al., 2019; Cai et al., 2019). The value of fish exports often surpasses that of other merchandise and agricultural exports, playing a pivotal role in shaping the growth trajectories of these countries (Asche et al., 2015; Béné et al., 2019) and contributing to food security through import capabilities (Marin et al., 2024).

Alsaleh (2023) underscored the critical role of fisheries in sustainable food security, introducing the concept of "blue food" and emphasizing the fishery industry's potential to address global food security challenges while maintaining ecological balance. While fish and fishery exports are generally believed to boost developing countries' economies and purchasing power (Golub and Verma, 2014; Asche et al., 2015), Emam et al. (2021) note that conclusive evidence supporting this claim remains limited.

Despite its potential, the fish export sector faces numerous challenges. These range from overfishing and inadequate infrastructure to compliance with stringent international quality standards (Bush et al., 2015). Sanitary and phytosanitary standards imposed by developed countries present significant barriers to export success (FAO, 2011), compounded by the necessity to conserve natural resources for future use (Muñiz et al., 2022). This scenario highlights the intricate balance required between economic expansion and environmental sustainability. Technological advancements, while beneficial in many aspects, have contributed to the depletion of wild fish stocks (Eigaard et al., 2014; Emam et al., 2021), posing challenges to maintaining healthy marine environments. The rapid growth of the aquaculture sector has brought its own set of environmental issues, including toxicological effects, eutrophication, bacterial resistance, and soil degradation (Ahmad et al., 2022). These problems stem primarily from the discharge of pollutants into water bodies from aquaculture operations (Liu et al., 2024). Alsaleh (2024a) highlights the widespread nature of water contamination issues in both developed and developing EU countries, emphasizing the global scale of this environmental challenge.

In response to these challenges, developing countries are implementing various strategic initiatives to maximize the benefits of fish exports while addressing environmental concerns, consumer health, and food safety. Examples include Ecuador's National Green Export Review (Emam et al., 2023; Ahmed et al., 2018; Chan et al., 2019; Chesnokova and McWhinnie, 2019; Ahmed, 2018). These measures aim to align with the standards of developed nations and support the Sustainable Development Goals (Emam et al., 2024). The impact of these initiatives on fish exports is complex, reflecting the dual-face hypothesis of standards (Medin, 2019). While imposing regulations can potentially hinder sector growth by limiting international market access (Nadarajah and Flaaten, 2017; UNCTAD, 2017), investments in research and development related to environmental and sustainability issues can enhance production efficiency (Xie et al., 2022). Understanding and addressing these multifaceted challenges is crucial for ensuring the sustained growth and resilience of the fish export sector in developing nations, extending beyond sustainable ocean management to broader global concerns (Kearney and Hilborn, 2022).

In light of these complexities, it is imperative to identify both external and internal barriers, as well as prospects for fish trade, to sustain the growth of the fishery industry in developing countries. This approach necessitates careful consideration of environmental issues in formulating effective policy strategies, recognizing the industry's significant role in the social and economic well-being of developing nations despite the challenges posed by global market standards and regulations.

To the best of our knowledge, only a limited number of studies have examined the potential impacts of trade in developing countries. These academic efforts aim to shed light on the current state of this sector within the economies of these nations and highlight both challenges and opportunities that could offer valuable insights for future growth. Notable research includes works by Akbari et al. (2023), Gambelli et al. (2019), and N'Souvi et al. (2024). However, these studies tend to focus on specific sectors or the global context, rather than providing a comprehensive analysis of the fish trade or exports in developing countries, where each nation possesses unique species with potential export benefits. Hence, it is crucial to view the fish trade sector holistically, rather than focusing on specific fish types or countries, to accurately depict the fisheries sector in developing nations.

As a result, the proposed study initiates a systematic literature review, centering on developing countries and their involvement in the fish trade, encompassing both exports and imports, without considering specific fish types, to present a holistic view of the sector in developing countries. It also examines the determinants and policies, specifically targeting Asian and other continental developing countries, excluding China, and landlocked developing nations. To enhance the relevance of this systematic study, it emphasizes several research questions in accordance with the guidelines stipulated by Waddington et al. (2012).

This study aims to address several key research questions: Does fish trade significantly contribute to the economic development of developing countries? What potential barriers and future opportunities do developing countries need to address strategically for fish trade? How does policy influence the fish trade in developing countries? What sustainable practices should be applied to ensure the viability of the fish trade? By addressing these questions, this research seeks to inform policymakers, concerned authorities, and the research community about the importance of fishery resources and their role in economic development. The findings may help reconcile various perspectives on the significance of the fisheries sector in developing nations and guide future policy decisions.

Systematic review approach
--------------------------

This study employed a comprehensive systematic review approach, conducted from June 10, 2023, to February 25, 2024. The methodology, adapted from Xiao and Watson (2019) and Linnenluecke et al., 2020a, Linnenluecke et al., 2020b, comprised four key stages: (1) identification of relevant studies, (2) selection of suitable publications, (3) data and information charting, and (4) summarization of findings for inclusion. Developing countries and global fish export market
--------------------------------------------------

Developing countries play a pivotal role in the global fish export market, significantly shaping the dynamics of international seafood trade (FAO, 2020; UNCTAD, 2017). Studies on fish export and economic growth, such as those by Emam et al. (2021), Gulob and Verma (2014), and Marin et al. (2024) emphasized the growing importance of developing nations in the global fish export market, which in turn contributes to their sustainable economic development (Tran et al., 2023). Discussion
----------

The global fish and fishery product export industry, particularly in developing countries, faces a critical juncture where sustainable practices are no longer optional but imperative. Unsustainable economic development not only presents ocean services but also jeopardizes future marine ecosystems and biodiversity (Phang et al., 2023). This situation calls for an urgent shift towards responsible resource management and environmental conservation (Pauly and Zeller, 2016). Alsaleh et al. (2023b)

Conclusion and policy implications
----------------------------------

This comprehensive review has examined the economic significance and prospects of fish exports in developing countries through a multifaceted analysis of the current economic landscape, challenges, opportunities, sustainable practices, and policy implications. The systematic literature review encompassed global seafood demand, economic impacts, sustainability issues, and relevant policy frameworks.

Abstract
--------

In this paper, a systematic literature review on the impact of fisheries on the economy and the application of the computable general equilibrium (CGE) and input–output (IO) methods for assessing this impact is conducted. The importance of fisheries as a food source, the over exploitation of this resource, and, consequently, the impact of fisheries on the economy are the motivations behind this study. By reviewing the applications of two of the most common economic modelling tools, we aim to shine light on the state of the art and how the impact of fisheries on the economy has been addressed in the literature. In this analysis, three main themes of socio-economic, ecological, and environmental have been identified, and the application of these methods in each theme has been considered. The results show that while IO methods continue to be applied in the literature, the CGE method has experienced increased application recently, and future applications are anticipated due to its enhanced capabilities in comparison with IO models.

Keywords: 

[input–output models](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=input%E2%80%93output+models); [fishery management](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=fishery+management); [CGE models](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=CGE+models); [regional economy](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=regional+economy); [economic modelling](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=economic+modelling); [sustainable development](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=sustainable+development)

1. Introduction
---------------

The fishery sector is one of the important contributors to coastal economies, with significant economic, political, social, and environmental effects within the regional economy. The fisheries are part of the wider economy of a region and involve a number of different industries and agents who facilitate the flow of products between producers and consumers. The interaction of the fishery industry with the economy could be illustrated as a flow involving several stages as following: (i) variety of fish products is produced by the primary fishery industry, (ii) the market intermediaries (i.e., wholesale and retail traders, processors, and brokers) will facilitate the movement of this production amongst households, processing industries, or forward-linked industries and export, (iii) fishers and forward-linked industries draw input from manufacturing and service industry or backward-linked industries, and (iv) governments and households are the key sectors that interact with the three industry category and connect these industries to the economy [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/15/7/6089#B1-sustainability-15-06089)].

In an open fishery access model, the fisheries are common property resources for which the right to catch fish is available to all without restriction or regulation [[2](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/15/7/6089#B2-sustainability-15-06089)]. However, given the fishers’ behaviour to maximize gains, overexploitation and environmental degradation, including depletion of fish population and the risk of extinction, would pose a threat from a sustainability perspective, which could also lead to negative economic consequences for the region. As stated by [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/15/7/6089#B3-sustainability-15-06089)], “It has been recognized for over 50-years that the root cause of the economic problem in capture fisheries management lies in their traditional common property nature and the resulting open access/near open access fisheries.”

From an economic standpoint, the maximum economic yield (MEY) in fisheries is defined as the point that generates the greatest margin of aggregate revenue over cost which incentivises others to enter the fisheries until the net benefit becomes zero, occurring at a point called Open Access Equilibrium (OAE). However, since OAE usually happens at levels of fishing effort greater than that required for maximum sustainable yield, it could often exhibit overfishing, resulting in potential economic impact on the economy of the region [[4](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/15/7/6089#B4-sustainability-15-06089)].

On the other hand, in a regulated open access model, such as the fisheries regulated under the European Common Fisheries Policy (CFP), fisheries are regulated via different measures, such as gear restrictions, seasonal restrictions, and total allowable catch [[5](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/15/7/6089#B5-sustainability-15-06089)]. Such measures are executed via state control, forms of co-management, and community-based management or privatisation. Nonetheless, these top-down management measures could also lead to poor results and dissatisfaction amongst fishers due to lack of transparency and prioritisation of the environmental objectives over criteria such as fisheries’ profit and local employment in the region [[6](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/15/7/6089#B6-sustainability-15-06089)]. Apart from the governance of fisheries (regulated or non-regulated), other external factors affect the fisheries; these factors include (i) environmental factors, such as climate change, (ii) ecological factors, such as changes in the fish biomass, and (iii) socio-economic factors, such as introduction of a new industry within the region.

Fisheries are often related to other sectors within the economy and can be divided into primary fishery sector (wild capture/aquaculture) and secondary fishery sector (processing and packaging, storage, transportation, etc.). To analyze the interdependence of these sectors and quantify the economic impacts of these sectors on each other and the wider economy, the economic models could determine measures, such as the cross-sectoral linkage effect, the employment induced effect, the production inducing effect, and supply shortage effects.

Therefore, the assessment of the impact of fisheries on the economy in light of the aforementioned factors becomes an important issue. In this paper, we present a literature review to consider the application of two well-known economic analysis tools, namely the input–output (IO) and computable general equilibrium (CGE) models, in quantifying and understanding the impact of interactions of fisheries within the regional/national economy. Although a significant amount of the literature is devoted to the assessment of the economy-wide impact of the fishery sector using IO and CGE methods, there is a paucity of recent systematic literature reviews on the application of these economic models in this sector, and the latest survey dates back to the year 2006 [[7](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/15/7/6089#B7-sustainability-15-06089)].

This study fills the gap by providing a comparative analysis and a literature survey on the aforementioned two well-known tools of economic systems modelling in terms of their assessment of the contribution of the fishery sector to the regional development covering the studies in the period between the years 2000 and 2020. The original contribution of this study is to characterize relevant studies in this domain and identify the current state of the art in the application of IO and CGE methods to assess the impact of the fishery sector on the economy, elaborate the strengths and weaknesses of these methods, and anticipate their future applications for the sector given the threat of overexploitation of marine resources. A systematic review will be more successful when the methodology is applied to a clearly defined research question on issues where a review seems sensible [[8](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/15/7/6089#B8-sustainability-15-06089)].

In this study, the following research questions are identified:

*   What are the main application categories of the CGE and IO methods to assess the impact of fisheries on the economy? 
*   Which type of criteria has been used in each method? 
*   What are the future trends in the application of CGE and input–output methods in the fishery sector? 

The remainder of this article is organised as following. In [Section 2](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/15/7/6089#sec2-sustainability-15-06089), the literature search process is explained. In [Section 3](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/15/7/6089#sec3-sustainability-15-06089), the theoretical ba

enriched_points.expanded_content: Increasing global demand for fish, driven by population growth and health trends, is reshaping the fisheries sector, emphasizing the need for sustainable practices to meet market needs.

Intensified competition among fishers leads to overfishing and resource depletion, threatening sustainability and economic stability in the fisheries sector, necessitating effective management strategies.

Illegal fishing undermines sustainable fisheries management, depleting stocks and harming ecosystems, while also creating unfair competition for legal fishers, complicating regulatory enforcement efforts.
